inline_comments.message:
I think HttpCodeFilter field should be optional. 
If there then use it otherwise fall back to the previous implementation (only 2XX are allowed). This will also help in keeping the backward compatibility.
Thoughts?

May be constant in some common place because the same needs to be used in Task for fetching the value.
Liam/John - May be you can suggest
No code changes in the file?
Better to use lombok for getters/setters.
IF there is a need to replace all getters and setters with the Lambok?
Ok. By default, the @setter annotation in Lombok is not possible to generate final filed. The only way is to override it with new setter function.
So I think the Lombok is not suitable here.
Better to take this to class level. And then it applies to all fields of the class.
It can be replaced by another lombok annotation @ToString at class level.
"[2][0-9][0-9] should be a default value in the RestClientCarrierTechnologyParameters class.
See previous comments, move code to here.
suggestion: since all fields have these annotations, could move them to class level
move this common code to a common superclass (or utility, if superclass is not an option)
same comment as before - can move these annotations to class level
need tests to ensure the filter works correctly?

Shouldn't this be removed or something?
Say, for e.g. when the HttpCodeFilter has [2,5][0-9][0-9], and the response status is 500, an exception will be thrown here itself, where we actually need to handle it.
I will delete the logic bug, thanks.
Yes, it is a logic bug, I will delete this filed.
Yes it is a task for POLICY-1884 later.
Filter works in consumer and the test is finished in other blocks.
This should be set as a default value on the field in the carrier technologies parameters class.
You need to validate the incoming HTTP return code pattern bere.
Maybe add test cases so that these things are caught.
Add nordix license in all files.
is it really necessary to sleep?  Perhaps use Awaitability to wait for some condition?  Or event better, use a latch that is automatically decremented when the condition is satisfied.

1. We can not delete filed httpCodeFilter and it comes from Json File. And the pattern is compiled from httpCodeFilter.
2. If we write the pattern in the properties, it will never changed even if the httpCodeFilter changed.
The pattern is not static. If we do like above, when the httpCodeFilter is changed, the Pattern will not change in my test, this is the reason why we have to compile it all the time.
How to validate the pattern or just simply validate if the httpCodeFIlternPattern is null.

Yes, that's a good question!

null is allowed because that means the expression is not specified. In that case set it to "[2][0-9][0-9]"

At least it should not be an empty string and it should compile.
Let's do that much for now.
Yes. I found this block I have wrote is useless, and return code test is covered in rest requestor test.
should probably do this in an "else" block attached to the following "if".  Otherwise, it will throw an NPE if the filter is null.
can filter really be null?  If so, then need to move line 100 into an "else" block attached to this "if".  In addition, could replace the two conditions with a single call to StringUtils.isBlank()
If filter can be null, then line 130 should be moved into an "else" block attached to this "if".  May also consider using StringUtils.isBlank().

Jim, you are correct. 

The filter can never be null because the contract from the properties class ensures that it is always set to the default value even if it is not specified in the incoming configuration.

Therefore, this entire if block is not required.
Please implement at least empty string check and that the pattern compiles.
StringUtil.isBlank() is a great way. I want leave this block and add a else function. Because some one may set it to null in some coding area.
We should put in a test that calls validate after this so that we can check if the validate on an invalid filter fails.
Remove this, it was just used for debugging.
is there a reason for using getUrl() on line 194, but this.url on line 199?
is this necessary?  If so, it might best be done in an @After method, to ensure that it's done, whether or not the junit test case fails in the middle.
this is fine, though the common code block could be done before the switch, and then just the appropriate method called within the switch

not sure why this is wrapped in a try/catch block - junit will generate an error if something throws an exception, so no need to add this extra code

same comment applies to other test cases
Try should be used here for init, and It can not be deleted.
I don't understand.  Just add "throws Exception" to the test case, then try/catch can be removed, while retaining lines 163-169
